planting.note: Plant rooted tip cuttings or bare-root canes in early spring and install a two-wire trellis at 3 and 5 ft at the same time.

directions: Tayberry is a Scottish-bred hybrid (blackberry x raspberry) with long, conical, deep purple-red berries that ripen in mid-summer; the flavor is richer and sweeter than loganberry. Train the long trailing canes fan-style along wires, keeping current-year primocanes separate from fruiting floricanes to simplify post-harvest cleanup. After harvest, remove all fruited canes to the ground and tie in the new primocanes. In zone 5–6, protect canes over winter by detaching them from wires and covering with straw mulch or a frost-cloth tunnel.

planting.note: Plant rooted tip cuttings in early spring after last frost; provide a sturdy T-trellis at planting since canes reach 8–10 ft.

directions: Loganberry, a natural hybrid of blackberry and raspberry discovered in California in the 1880s, produces long, dark red, tart berries that are excellent for jams and wine. Train the vigorous trailing canes on a two- or three-wire trellis, weaving new primocanes along the wires during the growing season. After the summer harvest, cut all floricanes that bore fruit to the ground and secure the new primocanes in their place. The thornless 'LY59' strain is preferred for home gardens; in borderline zones, lay canes down and mulch after leaf drop.

planting.note: Plant bare-root or tip-rooted canes in early spring as soon as soil is workable, with a T-trellis capable of handling 8-ft canes installed at the same time.

directions: Marionberry is a trailing blackberry hybrid (Chehalem x Olallie) developed by Oregon State University and is the most widely grown blackberry in the Pacific Northwest, prized for its deep, complex, slightly tart flavor. The thorny, vigorous canes must be trained along a two-wire trellis; weave new primocanes along the lower wire during the summer and tie them to the upper wire the following spring. After the mid-summer harvest, remove all fruited floricanes at the base and train the new primocanes into their place. Marionberries are sensitive to freezes below about 10°F; in colder areas, protect canes over winter.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings or transplants in early spring; note that wineberry is invasive in much of the eastern U.S., so check local regulations before planting.

directions: Wineberry, native to Japan and China, produces small, brilliant red, jewel-like drupes with a sweet, delicate flavor; it is grown for fruit production in Asia and has naturalized extensively in the eastern U.S. and should be grown only in contained settings or where legal. The canes are covered in red glandular hairs and tip-root readily, so remove spent canes promptly and clear any rooted tips. Harvest when berries are uniformly bright red and detach easily from the sticky calyx — they do not ripen after picking and must be used within 1–2 days. Pruning follows the same floricane/primocane cycle as standard raspberries.

planting.note: Plant bare-root canes in early spring; keep well away from other Rubus species to limit virus transmission.

directions: Black raspberries are erect, arching canes that propagate by tip layering rather than suckering; they have a richer, more intense flavor than red raspberries and are popular for jams and wines. Tip-prune primocanes at 24–30 in in midsummer to force lateral branching, then shorten those laterals to 8–12 in the following late winter for maximum fruit bud development. Remove all floricanes after harvest and any tips that have rooted outside the row. Do not plant near red raspberries or wild Rubus, as black raspberries are highly susceptible to aphid-spread viruses.

planting.note: Plant bare-root canes in early spring; most golden raspberries are primocane-fruiting and will yield a fall crop in their first year.

directions: Golden raspberries are yellow-fruited mutations of red raspberry cultivars, most commonly primocane-fruiting (everbearing) types such as 'Anne' or 'Fall Gold', with exceptionally sweet, mild flavor and low acidity. Manage as you would a primocane-fruiting red raspberry: for the simplest system, mow all canes to the ground each late winter and allow new primocanes to grow and fruit in fall. For a two-crop system, retain canes over winter to produce a summer crop on floricanes. Harvest when berries turn fully golden-yellow and release easily; they are more fragile than red types and should be processed quickly.

planting.note: Plant divisions or rooted cuttings in spring in moist, humus-rich soil; salmonberry naturalizes readily in Pacific Northwest conditions.

directions: Salmonberry is a Pacific Northwest native shrub that thrives in moist to wet, shaded sites along streams and forest edges, making it one of the few fruiting canes suitable for boggy ground. Fruits ripen very early — often in May or June — in shades from yellow-orange to deep red-salmon, with a mild, slightly bland flavor best suited to fresh eating or jams. Thin old canes to the ground each year after fruiting to maintain vigor, as the plant spreads by rhizomes and can form dense thickets. Wildlife value is extremely high; berries attract birds and bears within days of ripening.

planting.note: Plant root divisions or container-grown plants in early spring in partial shade with moist, well-drained soil.

directions: Thimbleberry is a large-leafed, thornless native Rubus that thrives along woodland edges and mountain slopes from Alaska to Mexico, tolerating more shade than almost any other fruiting cane. The soft, bright red berries resemble a wide, shallow thimble and have a delicate, sweet flavor but are extremely fragile — they must be eaten or processed within hours of harvest. Propagate by dividing root clumps in spring; seed germination is erratic and requires cold stratification. Allow the plant to naturalize in a semi-wild planting rather than managing it as a formal fruiting cane.

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions in spring in acidic, peaty, boggy or moist woodland soil; requires both male and female plants for fruit set.

directions: Cloudberry is a low-growing, rhizomatous Arctic-boreal species that requires cold winters, strongly acidic peat or sphagnum-rich soil (pH 3.5–5.0), and consistent moisture — it is exceptionally difficult to cultivate outside its native subarctic habitat. It is dioecious, so both male and female plants must be planted in proximity for fruit to set. The amber-golden berries are among the most highly prized wild fruits in Scandinavia and are used in traditional preserves; ripe berries are extremely soft and must be processed immediately. Not suited to continental climates south of USDA zone 5.

planting.note: Plant divisions or plugs in early spring in strongly acidic, humus-rich, moist soil, ideally under light tree canopy.

directions: Bilberry is a low-growing European relative of blueberry that differs in producing berries singly rather than in clusters; the deep purple-red flesh (unlike blueberry's white flesh) stains heavily and has an intense, slightly tart flavor. It demands strongly acidic soil (pH 4.0–5.0) with high organic matter and cool, moist conditions, making it challenging to grow in most American gardens but worth attempting in zones 3–7 under high shade or at elevation. Minimal pruning is needed; shear plants lightly every 3–4 years to rejuvenate. Harvest in mid- to late summer when berries are fully dark blue-black and tender.

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant after all frost danger has passed, spacing plants 24–30 in apart.

directions: Garden huckleberry (Solanum scabrum) is an African-origin annual grown for its jet-black, blueberry-sized fruit, which must be cooked before eating as raw berries contain solanine. Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost at 75–80°F and transplant outdoors after frost danger has passed. The bushy plants are prolific producers and generally need no staking. Harvest when all berries in a cluster have turned uniformly shiny black; cook with sugar and lemon juice to reduce bitterness and break down solanine before use in jams or pies.

planting.note: Plant hardwood cuttings or bare-root stock in early spring; jostaberry is self-fertile and a single plant will produce fruit.

directions: Jostaberry is a thornless hybrid of black currant and gooseberry, combining the vigor and disease resistance of both parents with a flavor profile between the two. It is significantly larger and more vigorous than either parent, and is notably resistant to gooseberry mildew and currant big bud mite. Prune as you would a black currant — remove one-third of the oldest canes each late winter to stimulate new productive growth. Harvest in mid-summer when berries have turned from red to very dark purple-black and developed a sweet, slightly musky flavor; verify local Ribes restrictions before planting.

planting.note: Plant hardwood cuttings or bare-root stock in early spring; white currants perform well trained as a cordon against a north- or east-facing wall.

directions: White currant is a yellow-fruited mutation of red currant sharing identical cultivation requirements: it fruits most heavily on spurs of two-year-old and older wood. The translucent, ivory-white berries are sweeter and milder than red currants and are prized for elegant fresh-eating and jellies. Prune each late winter to maintain 8–12 canes of mixed ages, removing canes older than 5 years. Harvest entire strigs when berries have turned fully translucent white-golden and taste sweet; check local Ribes restrictions before purchasing.

planting.note: Plant hardwood cuttings or bare-root stock in early spring; highly adaptable to dry, alkaline soils unlike most Ribes.

directions: Clove currant (Ribes odoratum, syn. R. aureum) is a tough, drought-tolerant native prairie shrub with intensely clove-spice-scented yellow spring flowers and glossy black berries in mid-summer. It tolerates alkaline, clay, or droughty soils far better than European currants, making it the best Ribes choice for the Great Plains and intermountain West. The berries range from mild to somewhat astringent depending on selection; look for named fruit-producing cultivars such as 'Crandall'. Prune by removing the oldest canes at the base every few years; the plant suckers moderately and can form a thicket if not managed. Verify state restrictions before planting.

planting.note: Plant one male plant for every 5–7 female plants in early spring; buffaloberry is wind-pollinated and both sexes must be within 50 ft.

directions: Silver buffaloberry is an extremely cold-hardy nitrogen-fixing shrub native to the Great Plains, valued for its bright red, tart berries that have historically been used by Indigenous peoples of North America for pemmican and sauces. It is dioecious and wind-pollinated, requiring male and female plants; purchase sexed plants, as seedlings cannot be sexed until flowering at 3–5 years. The thorny, silver-leaved plants thrive in alkaline, dry, or infertile soils where other fruits fail. Harvest by spreading a tarp and gently shaking or beating branches after a light frost, which softens the bitterness; berries are very tart raw but excellent cooked.

planting.note: Plant in early spring; goumi is partially self-fertile but yields improve with cross-pollination from a second plant.

directions: Goumi is a nitrogen-fixing shrub from East Asia that produces abundant small, cherry-red, speckled fruits with a sweet-tart flavor reminiscent of cherry. It is remarkably drought-tolerant once established and fixes atmospheric nitrogen through root nodules, making it a valued plant in permaculture systems. Prune lightly after fruiting to shape the plant and encourage denser branching; heavy pruning stimulates excessive vegetative growth at the expense of fruit. Harvest when berries are fully red and slightly soft — ripe fruits have little to no astringency and taste excellent fresh or dried.

planting.note: Plant in spring in any well-drained soil; autumn olive is highly invasive in the eastern U.S. — confirm it is legal in your state before planting.

directions: Autumn olive is a nitrogen-fixing Asian shrub with extremely high yields of small red-speckled berries that are rich in lycopene and have a sweet-tart flavor; it is among the most productive fruiting shrubs per acre in temperate climates. However, it is considered an invasive species across much of the eastern U.S. and is prohibited or discouraged in many states — verify legal status in your jurisdiction before planting. Named fruit-producing selections such as 'Garnet' or 'Red Wing' are more compact and productive than seedlings. Harvest by hand-stripping clusters in fall when berries are fully red; fruit quality improves after light frost.

planting.note: Plant two or more named cultivars for cross-pollination in spring in sandy, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil.

directions: Beach plum is a native Atlantic coastal shrub that thrives in sandy, salt-spray-exposed sites where few other fruits survive, making it invaluable for coastal and xeric gardens. The small (0.5–1 in), round fruits range from red to purple to blue at maturity in late summer and are traditionally used for preserves and jams on Cape Cod and Long Island. Plant at least two named cultivars such as 'Autumn' and 'Premier' for adequate cross-pollination, as seedlings are variable and often infertile. Prune lightly after flowering to maintain shape; heavy pruning reduces fruiting the following year.

planting.note: Plant two or more plants for cross-pollination in early spring; extremely adaptable to cold, dry, or alkaline sites.

directions: Nanking cherry is one of the hardiest fruiting shrubs available, reliably surviving temperatures below -40°F and tolerating poor, dry, or alkaline soils. The abundant small red cherries ripen in early summer and have a sweet, slightly tart flavor excellent for jams, juices, and fresh eating. Two or more plants are needed for cross-pollination; plants flower very early in spring and may need frost protection of blossoms in zones 3–4 during cold snaps. Prune after harvest to remove dead wood and open the canopy; the shrub is generally very healthy with minimal pest and disease problems.

planting.note: Plant grafted named cultivars in early spring in moist to wet, acidic, bottomland soil for best fruit production.

directions: Mayhaw is a small flowering hawthorn tree native to swampy bottomlands of the Gulf South, prized for its tart red fruits — ripening in May — that make exceptional jelly of deep pink color. It is well adapted to poorly drained, acidic, wet soil and is often planted along pond edges or low spots. Grafted cultivars such as 'Big Red', 'Super Berry', and 'Texas Star' bear much more reliably and earlier than seedlings. Fruit is harvested by spreading tarps under the tree and shaking, or by netting floating fruit from ponds in traditional southern production.

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost; seeds are very small and should be pressed onto the soil surface without covering.

directions: Alpine strawberries are compact, clumping, runner-free plants that produce small but intensely fragrant and flavored berries continuously from early summer through fall frost. Seeds are tiny and require light for germination — surface sow in trays and do not cover; keep moist at 65–75°F. Unlike garden strawberries, alpines form clumps rather than runners and are best propagated by division every 3–4 years or from seed. They are more shade-tolerant than hybrid strawberries, making them excellent for edging partially shaded paths. Harvest individual berries when fully red and aromatic.

planting.note: Plant bare-root crowns in early spring with crowns level at the soil surface; must be grown near standard red strawberries for adequate pollination.

directions: Pineberry is a white-fruited strawberry cultivar (largely derived from the South American species Fragaria chiloensis crossed with F. x ananassa) that produces pale cream-white berries with red achenes and a distinctive pineapple-like aroma when fully ripe. Pineberries have low self-fertility and require a red-fruiting strawberry variety planted nearby as a pollinator for adequate fruit set. Grow exactly as you would a june-bearing or everbearing strawberry, removing blossoms the first season to build root strength. Harvest when berries are white to pale pink with red seeds and give off a sweet pineapple fragrance.

planting.note: Plant after last frost with a very sturdy pergola or trellis; self-fertile cultivars such as 'Issai' are suitable for single-plant planting.

directions: Kiwiberry is a compact-fruited selection of hardy kiwi (Actinidia arguta) grown specifically for smooth-skinned, grape-sized fruits that are eaten whole without peeling — they have the same flavor as standard kiwifruit but ripen 3–4 weeks earlier. Self-fertile cultivars such as 'Issai' and 'Ken's Red' allow single-plant production, but yields are higher when a male pollenizer is present. The extremely vigorous vines require a heavy-gauge trellis or pergola capable of bearing hundreds of pounds of vine and fruit; prune hard each dormant season to renewal spurs. Harvest when fruits yield to gentle pressure and taste sweet with no starchiness.

planting.note: Plant one male per 4–6 female vines after last frost; protect new shoots from cats, which are strongly attracted to kolomikta vines.

directions: Actinidia kolomikta is the hardiest kiwi species, tolerating temperatures to -40°F in zone 3, and is grown for its small (1 in), sweet, smooth-skinned fruits that ripen in late summer. The vine is ornamentally striking due to pink-and-white variegated foliage on male plants. It is dioecious and requires both male and female plants — one male per 4–6 females. Cats are intensely attracted to the plants and will damage young vines, so protect new plantings with wire cages. Prune each dormant season to maintain a framework of lateral arms on a sturdy arbor or fence.

planting.note: Plant after last frost in full sun with a trellis or fence; roots are cold-hardy to zone 6 and re-sprout from the roots each spring.

directions: Maypop (Passiflora incarnata) is a North American native passionflower that is cold-hardy to zone 6, distinguishing it clearly from the tropical Passiflora edulis (passionfruit). The ornamental lavender flowers are stunning and the egg-sized yellow-green fruits have sweet, aromatic pulp similar to tropical passionfruit. Plants die back to the roots in zone 6–7 winters and re-sprout vigorously in late spring — do not remove the crown before new growth appears. Plant cross-pollinators nearby (more than one plant) for reliable fruit set. Harvest when fruits turn yellowish-green and fall or detach easily.

planting.note: Plant container-grown stock in spring after frost in a sheltered site; appreciates afternoon shade in hot climates.

directions: Chilean guava (Ugni molinae) is a compact, evergreen myrtle-family shrub native to Chile, grown for its small, crimson, aromatic berries with a flavor often described as strawberry-vanilla-bubblegum. The Queen of England's personal favorite berry, it is widely grown in the UK and mild coastal climates. It tolerates some frost but is best suited to USDA zones 8–10 in the ground, or grown in containers and overwintered frost-free. Prune lightly after harvest to maintain shape; no heavy pruning is needed. Harvest in late fall and winter when berries are deep red-purple and fully aromatic.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings or transplants in spring in humus-rich, strongly acidic, moist woodland soil in full shade.

directions: Partridgeberry is a native North American groundcover for shaded, acidic woodland gardens, forming a dense mat of evergreen, trailing stems barely 2 in tall. The bright red, twin-fused berries persist through winter and are edible but bland and seedy, making them more valuable as wildlife food and ornamental ground cover than for human harvest. Each berry is the fused product of two twin flowers — visible as two distinct calyxes on mature fruit. Grow in strongly acidic soil (pH 4.5–5.5) under deciduous trees; it is slow to establish but extremely long-lived with virtually no maintenance needed.

planting.note: Plant container-grown divisions in spring in moist, strongly acidic, well-drained soil under conifer canopy.

directions: Salal is an evergreen native shrub of the Pacific Northwest coast, found beneath conifers from central California to Alaska, where it forms dense thickets on acidic, well-drained forest soils. The dark blue-purple berries are similar in size to a blueberry but have mealy, mildly sweet flesh and were an important food source for coastal Indigenous peoples, eaten fresh or dried into cakes. Salal is propagated by division or from container-grown nursery stock; seed germination is slow and unreliable. The glossy foliage is commercially valuable in the floral trade, and home growers can cut leathery stems for arrangements without harming the plant.
